hints.to wander: 'To wander' is 'errare' — 'erat' is from 'sum, esse' (to be)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'est' — 'erat' with -era- is imperfect

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edo' (to eat)
hints.erro, errare, erravi, erratum: These are the parts of 'erro' (to wander/err)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'hominis' ends in -is

hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular would be 'iusti' — 'iustus' is nominative
hints.vocative singular: Vocative would be 'iuste' — 'iustus' is nominative as a predicate adjective
hints.accusative plural: Accusative plural would be 'iustos' — 'iustus' is nominative singular

hints.3rd declension adjective: 3rd declension adjectives have forms like -is, -e — 'iustus, -a, -um' is 1st/2nd
hints.4th declension: Adjectives do not follow the 4th declension

steps.prompt: What part of speech is 'hic' here?

hints.adverb (here): 'Hic' can be an adverb meaning 'here', but here it modifies 'homo' as a demonstrative
hints.relative pronoun: Relative pronoun would be 'qui' — 'hic' is a demonstrative pronoun

steps.prompt: Why does the centurion use 'erat' (imperfect) rather than 'fuit' (perfect)?
steps.target: imperfect describes an ongoing state — 'this man was (always) righteous'
choices: imperfect describes an ongoing state — 'this man was (always) righteous'
there is no difference in meaning
'fuit' would mean he is no longer righteous
erat is the only past tense of 'sum'
steps.reveal: The imperfect emphasizes the enduring quality of his righteousness

hints.there is no difference in meaning: The imperfect stresses duration/ongoing state, while perfect would be a simple past fact
hints.'fuit' would mean he is no longer righte: 'Fuit' marks a simple past fact — 'erat' more strongly emphasizes the ongoing nature
hints.erat is the only past tense of 'sum': 'Sum' has both 'erat' (imperfect) and 'fuit' (perfect) as past tenses
